rolloutContext.note: 100% rollout went live 2026-05-07. This snapshot pulls two parallel windows: the full cumulative since experiment start (Apr 30+, 9 days) and the post-rollout subset (May 7+, ~1.5 days).

indieClassification.headline: Zero indie signups in the experiment population — all 95 new barbers are Rental or Commission
indieClassification.definition: An indie is identified by CONTEXT_TRAITS_ROLE = 'barber' AND CONTEXT_TRAITS_USER_TYPE IS NULL in the IDENTIFIES table. Rental and Commission are explicit user_type values used for shop-employed barbers; indies have no shop relationship and therefore no user_type set.

experimentBreakdown.method: Cross-joined ACCOUNT_CREATED users with IDENTIFIES traits using ever-had logic (any timestamp). MAX_BY produced misleading nulls because user_type is sometimes transient at re-identify time.

tempPasswordCorrection.reality: All Flow B email_check temp_password results map to confirmed Rental/Commission barbers — they get temp_password from their shop owner. Indies do not show up via temp_password.
tempPasswordCorrection.impactOnNarrative: The 33.5% temp_password share remains the strongest Flow B signal (catching invited shop barbers before they create duplicate accounts), but it should NOT be described as 'catching indies'. It is catching shop-employed barbers who were already provisioned.

globalContext.interpretation: Indies exist as a population (1,641 active in IDENTIFIES last 90d) but the ACCOUNT_CREATED event was only instrumented at experiment deploy (2026-04-30), so we have no pre-experiment baseline of indie creation rate. All 1,641 indies are pre-existing accounts, not new signups during our window.

implications: The north-star metric 'reduction of accidental indie signups in Flow B' cannot be measured in this experiment because zero indies are being created in this traffic.

Possible explanations: (a) organic indie volume is genuinely low post-deploy; (b) indies typically sign up via web, not iOS/Android; (c) the experiment population (logged-out users on iOS/Android) does not include the indie acquisition channel.
Actionable: ask Eric/Yurii whether indie signups historically come through iOS/Android at meaningful volume. If not, we may need a different success metric for this experiment.

northStarData.note: flowB barber/signup_link = 1 user (instrumentation leak from PR #4143 not yet merged)

variantBalance.srmStatus: more skewed than cumulative (73/27) — full-rollout traffic appears worse, watch over coming days

northStarData.note: Still 0 indie in either variant; flowB barber/signup_link leak still active (1 user)

srmInvestigation.headline: SRM 73/27 confirmed real, not a data artifact — but new question: flowB has 3% logged-in rate vs flowA 56%

byDay.takeaway: Split is consistent across both post-100% days (73-74/26-27). Not a one-day fluke.

byAppVersion.takeaway: Post-100% has zero legacy version contamination — every device is on 3.23.0. The SRM is NOT explained by 'old versions only emit flowA'. It's a real LD bucketing skew.

loginRate.takeaway: flowB has 3% logged-in rate (3 distinct USER_IDs out of 103 devices) vs flowA's 56% (155/278). Suggests flowA is being assigned to existing logged-in users, while flowB catches mostly anonymous/first-time devices. This points to a real LD bucketing bias (or LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED firing on contexts where flowA users are already logged in).

tempPasswordInvestigation.headline: temp_password share dropped 36.9% → 23.0% (email-only). Real drop — likely 'backlog catch-up' not noise.

rampVsPost100.takeaway: temp_password dropped from 36.9% (168 events) in the ramp to 23.0% (28 events) in post-100%. Returning rose 47.9% → 59.8%. Hypothesis: the ramp window caught a backlog of invited barbers who had been holding their temp_password emails for days/weeks before exposure to Flow B; once that backlog cleared, post-100% reflects steady-state organic mix. Sample is small (4 distinct users with temp_password in 1.5 days) — confirm at week 3.

inputTypeMix.takeaway: Username share rose slightly (7.9% → 11.0%). Adds a small effect — but B2 already filtered to email-only, so the temp_password drop is genuine, not just an input_type mix artifact.

notes.delta_takeaway: Real LD bucketing skew, not a legacy-version artifact (post-100% is 100% on 3.23.0). Stable across both post-100% days. NEW finding: flowB has 3% logged-in rate vs flowA's 56% — see SRM investigation block.
Stable across windows. Flow B conversion lift over Flow A holds (~8-9 pp).

Real drop. Most likely backlog catch-up: ramp days 1-7 picked up invited barbers who'd been holding temp_password emails before Flow B existed; post-100% is steady-state organic flux. Returning share rose proportionally (47.9% → 59.8%). Sample still small (4 distinct temp_password users in 1.5 days).
Both rates dropped uniformly. Flow B still ~2× Flow A — the gap (and direction) persists.

Frustration tapping continues. Post-100% pace (59 in 1.5 days ≈ 39/day) is slightly above cumulative (278/9 ≈ 31/day). Microsoft Clarity will help us understand the post-tap behavior — see CLARITY_IOS_PROMPT.md.

notes.metric: Login abandoned at email_entry (Flow B new step)
notes.delta_takeaway: Continues to grow proportionally with volume. Post-100% rate aligns with prior days. Clarity recording will help us see what users do at this step.
Leak still active until PR #4143 merges. Rate is roughly stable (~1 event/day).

openQuestions: Why is flowB's logged-in rate only 3% (3/103 devices) vs flowA's 56% (155/278) in post-100%? Suggests LD assigns flowA mostly to existing logged-in users while flowB catches anonymous/first-time devices. Confirm by checking when LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires (cold start? login screen only?) — if it fires on cold start for already-logged-in flowA users, that explains the SRM mechanically.

Indie signup channel: does meaningful indie volume historically come through iOS/Android, or do indies sign up via web? If web, this experiment can't measure the indie north star — need a different success metric. Ask Eric/Yurii.

Forgot password Flow A: 278 events / 13 distinct users in 9 days = ~21 events per affected user. The frustration tapping pattern is real and concentrated. UX review of the non-dismissible sheet recommended; Clarity recording will pinpoint why users tap repeatedly.
resolvedThisSession: Indie identifier: role='barber' AND user_type IS NULL. Zero indies in the experiment population.
temp_password ≠ indie: all temp_password Flow B users are confirmed Rental/Commission shop barbers.
SRM 73/27 is consistent across days, not driven by legacy app versions (post-100% is 100% on 3.23.0).
